speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five of the interview with Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. You were describing to us the conditions and the situation and-- as the war progressed. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe we stopped at the [INAUDIBLE] Clou, where they let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was your second time you'd been caught in the net?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the second time, the second time--

speaker: interviewer
text: So an assembly Lager, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh, again, assembly Lager. Let us go. And we had to go back to the labor exchange. And that time, we were assigned to a, a, a firm that had demolition work, actually a firm that did demolition work during peacetime, and now, of course, did demolition work to bombed-out buildings. And we-- I started out there. My father, at the same time, worked at the-- at a factory and then also wound up at the demolition work. And eventually we came together one more-- by chance, they put two demolitions work teams together. And my father was on one. Yes, we worked out in the western, western part of Berlin. And what noteworthy is, what we had, we had engineers and chemists and lawyers. And they had to work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now there were a number of young high-schoolers. Well, number one, they had to explain certain words because the foreman was not Jewish. And it was usually some pretty rough language. And we simply didn't know certain four-letter words, can you imagine? But without blinking an eye-- batting an eyelash, they explained what it was. But anyhow, what then came. The young people don't learn anything. What are we going to do? We have to do something to keep them active.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what we do, we had to-- had some wheelbarrows, which had to be filled up with rubble and then had to-- we took the wheelbarrow to a place where we got rid of it. Anyhow, while the wheelbarrows were filled, we would give them a question. For instance, name all the-- name the lakes of the United States or certain mountain ranges, or even give them some simple mathematics problem. And when you came back with the wheelbarrow, you had to have the answer, this kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these were the older, professional men were asking you these.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they realized-- there are the young people. They don't go to-- do anything. We have to do something about it at least. And what they did were questions being asked. Was quite interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was quite a work-study program.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or we would recite some poetry or something. It was quite nice, see. And if you didn't know, they told us. Therefore, the education-- the people realized something has to be done. The young people can't sit around here and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with wheelbarrows full of rubble.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this where you acquired your taste for opera?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was later. Actually, during that time, they caught us once again and put us in a transit camp. What happened, they needed probably some people for a transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how do we get him? The Jews have to pick up their ration cards. We're going to park outside and catch him. Lo and behold, I was caught again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are put-- was Rosenthaler Street. And they-- we kept-- two days they kept us inside. Afterwards, I found out there was a demonstration of the non-Jewish partner wives to get us out. But there was no way out of there. They kept us a couple of days in there, and then let us go, again, because of the mixed marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for you, this has rescued you now three times--

speaker: interviewee
text: Again.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from very close to being deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so we knew quite well what happens to people who stayed down there. They let us go. And we got bombed out again. And we moved to the north of Berlin. And actually, there, at that time, my father and I were the same work gang. And we usually came home at night, and there was no hot water and no central heating. We had to bring the wood, actually cut up wood and brought it home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to-- we, sometimes we'd cut up wood that was still usable. And lo and behold, one day comes the area supervisor that said, how could you do that? I'm going to have you deported. I'm doing what to you? I couldn't figure out what got into him. He was quite decent. And there was the local super, what they call him. He was standing there watching. Once that super left, he said, is he gone? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, let me tell you something what I had to do with. He come to me and denounced you. He told me that you'd use that wood-- window frames and stuff, good, nice, hard wood, good for burning. He came to me and denounced you. If I hadn't really make a big scene here, I don't know what would happen. He might have called the Gestapo. Therefore, be very careful. He is so nice and friendly. But he is just out to do you in, see. There it is. I don't mean it, certainly not. But there. But I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go on, I meant to ask you how many hours a day did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 to 12. They even gave us special ration cards for long-- for the long hours. 10 to 12, sometimes even longer, and Sundays, too, at times. But they still let us go home at night. It was still-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of food were you eating to sustain you through so much hard work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks goodness to my aunt that was in a much better position. Sometimes they actually have brought us some soup for some soup kitchen. Everyone working demolition work got it. We got it, too. It was pretty thin. Sometimes the noodles were on the bottom. But it was something warm. But otherwise, we had to make do with what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, your father was working alongside of you. How old was he at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was born in '86. I can figure out. It was in '43. '40-- Yeah, 43-- 50-- in his--

speaker: interviewer
text: In his late 50s?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, late 50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is quite arduous work for someone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, however, one good thing-- he had diabetes before. Through the war, the diabetes disappeared because of the, well, the little sugar, and not enough to eat. I personally never really-- yes, we were hungry. What we did, actually, we made up menus, what we would eat when it was all over. And like-- that we did. But particularly-- I, personally, thanks to my aunt, really never was any close to starving. I was hungry, yes, but not really deep hunger. Yes, I learned to eat stuff I didn't like but that we have-- fried potatoes, either in salt or coffee grounds, mineral oil, or castor oil. Or eat potato peels, fried nicely. And so this kind of stuff, you got used to it. And the bread was dreadful. And if they got some butter, and you warmed it, it separated in four different parts-- sections of oil in there. But frankly, you got used to this. But thanks to my aunt, I was particularly fortunate. I will say I was extremely fortunate, obviously.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during that time, of course, no radios allowed. But I still had the radio, a little crystal set. Of course, crystal set couldn't be traced. It didn't make any noise. The neighbors didn't hear it. And who knew we have it in the-- you had it under-- in a shoebox under your bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we lived all in one room. Lo and behold, one night they said, there's an opera tonight. I had heard the opera arias, and my mother and father had talked about it. But I never even heard an opera. Well, I said, maybe tonight I am lucky. There will be no air raid and I can listen to it. Lo and behold, comes 8 o'clock-- well, no, 9 o'clock what it was-- late-- usually every hour on the hour, they told you are there any planes coming in. No, the Reich is free of enemy planes. Oh, OK. Well, I'm going to sit here and put the thing-- I'm going 15, 10 minutes to see what an opera is like. I put it on. And I didn't put them off until the opera was over. And I remember distinctly-- the same performance I heard there, I still have now on the CD, that historical performance. I can play it now. I have played it. It was That's the first time I actually heard a-- it was fascinating.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have a CD of that actual performance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of that, actually, you see. It was a recorded performance, a very fine one. And I listened to it. And lo and be-- I remebered who was singing, what it was. Lo and behold, I saw it. The year was 1943. This was one of the last perform-- theaters were closed already and the opera house. But what they had, it was in the radio orchestra still played, just for the radio. And there was some opera singers who sang. And lo and behold, I have it here. I play it. It's very interesting. What strikes me is the German's a little bit funny. But I still remember some of the German words. And I know them now in German and Italian. But some, I didn't realize, quite slow. But the performance is still there. But that's the way I got hooked with opera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in any other special details or do different kinds of slave labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, actually what happened-- we were bombed out again. Actually, what happened is they put us in a detail-- it out-- actually bizarre. They took us off-- pulled us off the demolition work and put us to work on building-- re-building the, the-- building the foundation of a section of Berlin that was to be established after the war. There was a-- there was going to be-- special buildings are going to be there. And there's going to be-- I don't know what you call it-- you know, a special section after the war. I mentioned that was in '43 or '44. And we had to work there. No longer demolition, foundation work for some-- imagine. In '44, they still thought of having a, a special section. They took us there, and then they took us away to build tank traps one night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly is a tank trap?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a kind of metal rods. You make this rod this direction. The tank comes apparently from this side and gets impaled on that rod.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, like in parking lots, if you go out the wrong way.

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit higher. Actually, it sticks out of the-- little bit higher above the ground. And they thought if there's a tank, for some reason, it gets-- well, as I said, we worked there. And I remember-- and what a joke already. Those traps had been built before. Only that time, they needed us more. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, lo and behold, the joke-- it's very nice. Now, how long will it take the Russians to overcome this? I'll tell you exactly. 31 minutes. Why 31 minutes? For 30 minutes, they're going to stop laughing right in front of it, and one minute to get through it. But with-- exactly what happened-- they rode right over it afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they let us go home. After the tank traps would be found, we were bombed out again. This one the north of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: From your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again where I lived, there where I got to just listen to the opera. Well, that was the second time. Lo and behold, they put us-- had to do something-- they put us into a hospital, a Jewish hospital, till they found new living space for us. And there, what happened, we were there. It was the headquarters, Eichmann's headquarter. And the [INAUDIBLE] was hit by some bombs. And they needed some extra people. Anyhow, lo and behold, I came out one morning. Oh, no, no, no, no, wait here! You, you, you, you out of that truck, up there. And that's the way I wound up for some time in Eichmann's headquarter. And this was quite interesting appearance that Eich--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the building I knew before. It was a Jewish office for I don't know what it was. Anyhow, Eichmann had taken over. Eichmann was in charge of the deportation of the Jews, of the Final Solution. And there we were. And it was quite interesting to-- there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of his rank--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and what his responsibilities were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we knew about-- Eichmann was, no, an official we knew quite well what he was doing. We knew what it was. In fact, I believe there were some op-- I'm quite sure they didn't catch me the first day, probably by some people in that truck already were before.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just happened to be caught there. What could I do? I had another job. But this was called 'catastrophe'-- what would you call it-- 'detail.' And that came first, the Gestapo. And who would argue with the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: And probably they wouldn't make it up anyhow. I couldn't go to my demolition work. I had to do the demolition work there at Eichmann's headquarter. It was quite interesting-- quite many of Czech SS. Usually the Czech and the Austrian were worse than the Germans. There, too, Eichmann-- Eichmann we didn't see very much. I saw him once. You could tell him, his uniform. He came down with the entourage. I was standing here, and he was sitting right there. He discussed something, what should be done there, whether they should repair this or not. Anyhow, he didn't even look at any one of us. The real force was the deputy, Gunther, Ralph Gunther. He's still around. They haven't caught him yet. He was very nasty. He had a big German shepherd and was running around, trying-- catching people taking a rest or over the balcony shouting, everybody get to work here, and so on. He was the one who-- he was mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some other guys. For instance, we worked in front, in front of a building with a guard, enlisted man. [INAUDIBLE], one enlisted man continuously cat-calling, where you get that? Hurry up! He [INAUDIBLE] us go. The other one, when they have him arrested, conveniently looked-- had something else to do. Two men, one decent, the other, a very nasty customer. Did you come in for much verbal abuse?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not too much. There was a certain unpleasant tension there. It was actually correct. There was no beating. There was no shouting.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, for instance, you have to do this. Or for instance, I moved the furniture for one of the Czech guys. And for some reason, I made the suggestion that perhaps we should move. Oh, can you see it? You couldn't really--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very uneasy situation, with one exception. There was one guy. He was a second lieutenant. The name is Haggenberg. I couldn't figure out then and can't figure out now how the men got there. The tension wasn't there. Not he was overly friendly, but there was not the tension. He was correct, not friendly. But for some reason, you could feel he didn't want to harass you. He would, actually-- wanted to make it easy on you. He was-- absolutely not one-- never raised his voice, not one improper word, anything. The man, to me-- for some reason, he actually liked me, put me to work. Do this and that. And when he came, he seemed genuinely concerned. I don't know what the man was doing there. Obviously, he didn't belong there. And I sometimes wonder, probably a young man got there, he couldn't help himself. He couldn't get out of it, I wonder.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a career officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently, he was an officer. He had that SD, [GERMAN], 'security service,' right on his sleeve. And he was there with the others. But he was the one guy who, for some reason, if I would meet him now, I would invite him to sit down with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To me and others, he did not one intemperate word, not one remark or anything else. Very-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you under any more stress than usual knowing that you were working right there in the headquarters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. You never knew. You never knew what those-- Gunther. Who knows, he might get the idea to-- what, maybe you did something wrong that he disliked. You never knew with those guys. Maybe he knew that he was-- and that there wasn't a permanent crew. I don't do what he did actually there-- only Jews there. And that's the reason after the war, [INAUDIBLE] I had enough. I said, no, things have died down. They did-- actually don't know me. I wasn't actually-- didn't have to check in by name. I tell you, I said to myself, tonight, I'm not going outside to that. I'm just going-- it's morning, it was-- I just go over the fence, and go back to my regular work. Well, I took a chance. But frankly, I didn't want to stay any longer in Eichmann's boat than I had to. And I went back, and nothing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Maybe Haggenberg could have reported me [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't. Therefore-- I sometimes wonder what happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: You will find a decent man even at the security service in Eichmann's headquarters. Yes, it happens-- sometimes circumstances beyond his control. Obviously, once he was in, he couldn't say, I won't like it anymore. He was a young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start getting the sense that things were going sour for the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that I can tell you. I'll never forget it. I was working at a different part. Lo and behold, it was an SS administrative office. I don't know what it was. Some SS-- I don't exactly what it was, either the barracks or something. Lo and behold, I was working and watching. They were loading a truck with furniture and boxes or something. Well, then it happened. They had to-- the SS had to push the truck. They didn't even have gas to move their own trucks. When I saw that, I said, that's it. It's over. Germany lost the war. If the SS doesn't have any gasoline for their trucks, they must be in very bad shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, I would say, a few months before the end. At that time, it was pretty close. Pretty soon after that, you could hear [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the guns from the Russian front. You already could hear. The end of the war approached. Of that, I remember the last, pretty close to end of the war, we worked at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we talking about late 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was late 1944. That's correct. And I thought, you really could hear the guns in the distance. And you work. And there was a closet with a storage house for meat and other-- meat, cheese, and stuff, they used to store. And what got hit, we had to clean up. It didn't help us very much, because all the meat was rotten, and so on. But anyhow, we did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And lo and behold, they come equipped with the guns. And indeed, my father comes to me, and said, you know, the overseer, hey, what just happened? He just addressed me as 'Your Honor,' or the equivalent in German was Your Honor. He probably realized it's coming to the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was right. And so it was actually, there the-- I would say, about a couple of weeks after that, the, the public transportation ceased to run. We stayed at our home, which we shared with two other families. Finally, they let us go into hospital-- to the western-- to apartment in West Berlin. Thanks goodness, it's going to be very important where you were at the end of the war, whether you were the east sector or west sectors. It was quite important. We stayed there. And the end of the war came. Again, fortunate, stay in the-- at the door, trying to get some water. You're waiting for a lull in the bombardment. And I was ready to go. Suddenly, bing! And the man next to me falls down, was hit by shrapnel or something. It could have been me standing here. But I was alive, got out, got some water. That was quite-- I don't-- by the time you came back, half of the bucket was empty because you had to go down again and up. Lo and behold, what happened, I was there and heard there's a bakery not-- down the street. They might have some bread. OK, I'm going to go, and actually got some bread. And suddenly, on the way back, I hear, hey, ho, stop! I look around. There were some Russian soldiers behind me. They wanted me to come back. But in front of me, out of our building, our apartment house, some other Russians came and said-- more or less motioned-- let him go, we have him here. And there I was. And of course, right then, they didn't believe me.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are a Jew? All Jews kaput! All Jews. Then I had to show actually my identification. And there it was. And actually, the front troops were very decent. What was your impression of the liberating Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the liberating-- I always have a soft spot for the Russians. You see, the main force came from the east, Mark-- Marshal Zhukov. And what I was being told, that already they got-- the SS got to the Jews in the north, rounding them up and killing them, or whether they did. But they didn't get to us because from the southern-- and Berlin. Marshal Konev, the one with a bald, shaven head, came in so fast. We didn't expect him. We were surprised to see him. He just came in. The Germans, everybody, we knew they come from the east. But now, there comes a second army. And the second army-- one army came from the south-- and Konev. When they came in time and hear him, the front troops were very good. What happened afterwards, the commissars, and-- it wasn't so good. They took whatever, the women, and going around. For instance, they wanted watches. The Russians, they had watches up to here, you see. Some had never seen-- no, I think it's true haven't seen it. But this-- one of our Jewish friends told me, their apartment-- they had never seen a flush toilet. They washed their face in the-- they'd never seen that-- Siberians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was right in some way. Said, when will the war be over? Well, he said, war will be over when Mongols and Negroes will shake hands at the Brandenburg Gate. And that is exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty soon afterwards, the Americans came in and occupied the American sector. And this was actually for the end of the war. But the post-war time was pretty rough, too. It wasn't just over.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so tell me what was the date that you would describe as the end of the war for you in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, I would say-- it must've been in March or April, just about that time in '45. That was the end of the war. I still remember there was still shooting, and there was some fighting around Berlin, too. My father and I went out for some errand. And lo and behold, there came a Russian soldier, a bottle in one hand, shouting, [GERMAN], meaning 'war is over.' And well, then, the bombardment ceased. And then the so-called post-war period started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we move on to the post-war, how did you discover about the death of Hitler in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards. And I still remember it was two or three days before the Russians came in. Papers were no longer published. But they had fliers. And they would say, at the gates of Berlin, the Russians will be defeated. You hold out, and we are going to beat them back. That was two days. The Russians were right outside the gates of Berlin. By the gates of Berlin, the Russians will meet their defeat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the siege of Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: The siege, I would say, it was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] probably a week-- you still had the gun, but really, bombardment. And people tell us, you are very fortunate that the Russians took Berlin. The Americans would just have pulled back and really bombarded it. But there you had house-to-house fighting, see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he saw, remember, out going for water. And there was a group of Mongolian transcripts, Russian, we were running. What do you think could have happened-- will have happened after the Russians caught him? They were the troops from General [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Russian general who fought for the Germans. And you could see the brigade-- or whether they called it, [INAUDIBLE]. They were scared. That-- I still remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the liberators treat any uniformed German soldiers and Nazis that they found?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not see that exactly. I assure you, at the time the Russians came, they took off their uniforms. That's the reason why they asked me, what are you doing here? Come, come, come, come, come. You-- all Jews kaput. You are not-- you are just a soldier or a Nazi. I said, prove it. Thanks goodness I had my-- they knew enough probably that-- actually, there was some German speaking. It was amazing, probably deliberately, the front lines by some warrior Germans, and so on. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Let me see, anything important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow, well, that whipped us through--
